story:
Me and Larry had our homebuilt go-kart pointed down the sidewalk, just waiting for the Tecumseh engine to kick and the belt to grab so one of us could blast past the DeWalls’ porch like a blur. Amaryllis and Belinda—twin sisters—were pretending not to watch us while they drank real lemonade with Dawn, Helen, Olive (who looks like a pimento olive if you squint), two new twin girls I called Rose and Reggie because their names were too long, and Yolanda. I didn’t like Yolanda. Larry did.

Mrs. DeWall waved us over for cookies and lemonade. I said no because cool guys doing cool go-kart stuff don’t sit with girls. Larry, who’s shallow as rainwater in a gutter, wanted to go. Mom says I should “get acquainted” with girls someday, but me and Larry were going to be racecar drivers, and Mom (who teaches grammar) said, “Larry and I.” I said that’s what I said.

We’d yanked the engine off my real dad’s old reel mower and built the kart behind our garage. The preacher in the house behind us—Reverend Doctor Gamble—but we call him Pastor Gumball because his head is round and bald—was always out there, fixing fences and blessing the neighborhood with long words. He’s got a fake leg from the war, and we try to be respectful when he’s around. Larry said “hot damn” once, Mom heard, and told him to go home; he sat on the back steps, told her his sad story, and still got peanut butter and jelly with milk anyway. He always knows what to say.

Next day we hauled the kart to the front sidewalk, safely out of Mom’s ears, and saw the girls again on the DeWalls’ porch. Larry got a look in his eye and said we should run a garden hose from the engine exhaust to the porch so they’d get sick and we could steal the cookies. Even I knew that was dumb and wrong. We tried to drag the kart and the hose across the lawn anyway, but before we got halfway, Mom hollered from the back steps, “You boys quit trying to run that hose from the exhaust to the DeWalls’ porch so those girls get sick and you can steal the cookies.” We dropped the whole idea. Nobody got hurt, except our pride.

I tightened the front two-by-four so the steering board wouldn’t flop when we hit the broken slab by old Mr. Sampica’s oak roots—right in front of the DeWalls’ place. I was the engineer; Larry was the helper. He’d rigged the engine on a hinge so up meant faster and down meant slower. Then he ditched me to do his Elvis Presley walk and croon at the girls, especially Yolanda. I got mad and cranked everything tight—real tight.

It was too hard to start the engine and then hop in. So I backed up, ran full speed in my new high-top Keds, jumped over the engine, and came down half-on the seat, half-still on my feet just as the motor fired. The clutch grabbed, the belt bit, and suddenly I was flying, still standing up, yelling, “Here I come!” I thumbed my ears and waggled my fingers like a hippo right as the kart hit the big crack by the oak. I heard the steering two-by-four snap, the world flip, and then I was on the sidewalk, everyone yelling not to move.

Mom came running. Mrs. D dropped the lemonade. Larry, my former best friend, said, “That was cool. The steering board’s busted. If you die, I’ll take care of the Tecumseh.” He told Mom my leg was probably broken and that he better go home. I was shaking under a beach towel and grinning like my face would split. Amaryllis leaned in, eyes big as wheels, not talking, just squeaking. I must’ve been delirious because I said, “Hot damn, that was cool.” Mom cried and told Mrs. D I’m a good boy, just out of my head. Maybe both were true. A year later, I know I was immature. But that day, busted leg and all, I was exuberant.
